{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
| name = Tsukamyojin Kofun (束明神古墳)
| image = File:束明神古墳 墳丘.jpg
| alternate_name = Sadatsukamyojin Kofun
| location = {{ill|Takatori|WD=Q1202681}}
| coordinates = 34 | coordinates = 27 | coordinates = 20.25
| coordinates = 135 | coordinates = 47 | coordinates = 10.88
| type = {{ill|Octagonal tumulus|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal tumulus|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
| area = Diagonal length: 30m
| burial = {{ill|Horizontal stone chamber with frontal entrance|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal stone chamber with frontal entrance|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}} (with a lacquered wooden coffin)
| era = Late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century}}
| designation1 = Not Historic Site1
| architectural_styles = {{ill|octagonal kofun|WD=Q11391751}}
}}

'''Tsukamyojin Kofun''' (束明神古墳) is an {{ill|octagonal kofun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/octagonal kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} located in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|en|Takatori, Nara|WD=Q1202681|lt=Takatori, Nara}}, Sada, Nara Prefecture. The tomb is not Historic Site1 as a historic site but is believed to be the true burial site of {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|WD=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe}}, Crown Prince to Emperor Tenmu.

== Overview ==
The tomb is located on the southern slope of the Mayumi Hills at the southern edge of the Nara Basin. Archaeological investigations were conducted between 1984 and 1985. 

The tumulus was constructed by leveling a large area of approximately 40 meters in diameter and building the mound using rammed-earth techniques. The tomb has an octagonal type with a diagonal length of approximately 30 meters. The burial facility is a {{ill|horizontal stone chamber with frontal entrance|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/horizontal stone chamber with frontal entrance|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}} opening to the south. The chamber is constructed using tuff-cut stones, with a rectangular floor plan and a trapezoidal cross-section. The interior likely contained a lacquered wooden coffin. burial goods such as human bones (teeth), iron nails, and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} were discovered during the excavation.

The tomb is dated to the late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century}}, making it a representative example of the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}. Based on its location, map_size, and construction, it is thought to be the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|WD=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe}}, Crown Prince to Emperor Tenmu.

== burial ==
The burial chamber features a unique horizontal stone structure:
* '''area:''' Length 3.06m; width 2.12m; estimated height 2.50m.
* '''Material:''' Rectangular tuff blocks measuring 50cm × 50cm × 30cm.
* '''built:''' The walls were built vertically up to a height of 1.27m, after which they were inclined inward at approximately 60 degrees, creating a trapezoidal cross-section.
* '''Interior:''' The floor was paved with two layers of stone, and a section of the floor was coated with plaster. 

burial goods recovered include more than 50 iron nails, lacquer fragments, and coffin fittings, suggesting the use of a lacquered wooden coffin. The presence of large nails implies that the coffin rested on a wooden plattype.

== Historical Context ==
The tomb is believed to be the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|WD=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe}}, who served as Crown Prince to Emperor Tenmu. Prince Kusakabe died prematurely in 689 AD, leaving a significant legacy through his descendants, including Emperor Monmu and Empress Genshō.

While the site is not officially Historic Site1 as a historic landmark, its map_size, construction, and historical significance make it an important archaeological site. The burial chamber has been reconstructed and is on display at the {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|WD=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute}} Museum.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|WD=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute}} Museum (Kashihara, Nara Prefecture): Reconstructed burial chamber from Tsukamyojin Kofun is on display.

'''Tsukamyojin Kofun''' (束明神古墳) is an {{ill|octagonal kofun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/octagonal kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} located in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|en|Takatori, Nara|WD=Q1202681|lt=Takatori, Nara}}, Sada, Nara Prefecture. The tomb is not Historic Site1 as a historic site but is believed to be the true burial site of {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|WD=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe}}, Crown Prince to Emperor Tenmu.

== Overview ==
The tomb is located on the southern slope of the Mayumi Hills at the southern edge of the Nara Basin. Archaeological investigations were conducted between 1984 and 1985. 

The tumulus was constructed by leveling a large area of approximately 40 meters in diameter and building the mound using rammed-earth techniques. The tomb has an octagonal type with a diagonal length of approximately 30 meters. The burial facility is a {{ill|horizontal stone chamber with frontal entrance|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/horizontal stone chamber with frontal entrance|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}} opening to the south. The chamber is constructed using tuff-cut stones, with a rectangular floor plan and a trapezoidal cross-section. The interior likely contained a lacquered wooden coffin. burial goods such as human bones (teeth), iron nails, and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} were discovered during the excavation.

The tomb is dated to the late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century}}, making it a representative example of the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}. Based on its location, map_size, and construction, it is thought to be the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|WD=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe}}, Crown Prince to Emperor Tenmu.

== burial ==
The burial chamber features a unique horizontal stone structure:
* '''area:''' Length 3.06m; width 2.12m; estimated height 2.50m.
* '''Material:''' Rectangular tuff blocks measuring 50cm × 50cm × 30cm.
* '''built:''' The walls were built vertically up to a height of 1.27m, after which they were inclined inward at approximately 60 degrees, creating a trapezoidal cross-section.
* '''Interior:''' The floor was paved with two layers of stone, and a section of the floor was coated with plaster. 

burial goods recovered include more than 50 iron nails, lacquer fragments, and coffin fittings, suggesting the use of a lacquered wooden coffin. The presence of large nails implies that the coffin rested on a wooden plattype.

== Historical Context ==
The tomb is believed to be the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|WD=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe}}, who served as Crown Prince to Emperor Tenmu. Prince Kusakabe died prematurely in 689 AD, leaving a significant legacy through his descendants, including Emperor Monmu and Empress Genshō.

While the site is not officially Historic Site1 as a historic landmark, its map_size, construction, and historical significance make it an important archaeological site. The burial chamber has been reconstructed and is on display at the {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|WD=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute}} Museum.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|WD=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute}} Museum (Kashihara, Nara Prefecture): Reconstructed burial chamber from Tsukamyojin Kofun is on display.

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}

| burial = {{ill|Horizontal stone chamber with frontal entrance|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horizontal stone chamber with frontal entrance|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}} (with a lacquered wooden coffin)

'''Tsukamyojin Kofun''' (束明神古墳) is an {{ill|octagonal kofun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/octagonal kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} located in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|en|Takatori, Nara|WD=Q1202681|lt=Takatori, Nara|1=Takatori, Nara}}, Sada, Nara Prefecture. The tomb is not Historic Site1 as a historic site but is believed to be the true burial site of {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|WD=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe|1=Prince Kusakabe}}, Crown Prince to Emperor Tenmu.

== Overview ==
The tomb is located on the southern slope of the Mayumi Hills at the southern edge of the Nara Basin. Archaeological investigations were conducted between 1984 and 1985. 

The tumulus was constructed by leveling a large area of approximately 40 meters in diameter and building the mound using rammed-earth techniques. The tomb has an octagonal type with a diagonal length of approximately 30 meters. The burial facility is a {{ill|horizontal stone chamber with frontal entrance|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/horizontal stone chamber with frontal entrance|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}} opening to the south. The chamber is constructed using tuff-cut stones, with a rectangular floor plan and a trapezoidal cross-section. The interior likely contained a lacquered wooden coffin. burial goods such as human bones (teeth), iron nails, and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} were discovered during the excavation.

The tomb is dated to the late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}}, making it a representative example of the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. Based on its location, map_size, and construction, it is thought to be the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|WD=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe|1=Prince Kusakabe}}, Crown Prince to Emperor Tenmu.

== burial ==
The burial chamber features a unique horizontal stone structure:
* '''area:''' Length 3.06m; width 2.12m; estimated height 2.50m.
* '''Material:''' Rectangular tuff blocks measuring 50cm × 50cm × 30cm.
* '''built:''' The walls were built vertically up to a height of 1.27m, after which they were inclined inward at approximately 60 degrees, creating a trapezoidal cross-section.
* '''Interior:''' The floor was paved with two layers of stone, and a section of the floor was coated with plaster. 

burial goods recovered include more than 50 iron nails, lacquer fragments, and coffin fittings, suggesting the use of a lacquered wooden coffin. The presence of large nails implies that the coffin rested on a wooden plattype.

== Historical Context ==
The tomb is believed to be the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|WD=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe|1=Prince Kusakabe}}, who served as Crown Prince to Emperor Tenmu. Prince Kusakabe died prematurely in 689 AD, leaving a significant legacy through his descendants, including Emperor Monmu and Empress Genshō.

While the site is not officially Historic Site1 as a historic landmark, its map_size, construction, and historical significance make it an important archaeological site. The burial chamber has been reconstructed and is on display at the {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|WD=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute|1=The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture}} Museum.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|WD=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute|1=The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture}} Museum (Kashihara, Nara Prefecture): Reconstructed burial chamber from Tsukamyojin Kofun is on display.

'''Tsukamyojin Kofun''' (束明神古墳) is an {{ill|octagonal kofun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/octagonal kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} located in {{ill|Takatori, Nara|en|Takatori, Nara|WD=Q1202681|lt=Takatori, Nara|1=Takatori, Nara}}, Sada, Nara Prefecture. The tomb is not Historic Site1 as a historic site but is believed to be the true burial site of {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|WD=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe|1=Prince Kusakabe}}, Crown Prince to Emperor Tenmu.

== Overview ==
The tomb is located on the southern slope of the Mayumi Hills at the southern edge of the Nara Basin. Archaeological investigations were conducted between 1984 and 1985. 

The tumulus was constructed by leveling a large area of approximately 40 meters in diameter and building the mound using rammed-earth techniques. The tomb has an octagonal type with a diagonal length of approximately 30 meters. The burial facility is a {{ill|horizontal stone chamber with frontal entrance|ja|横口式石槨|1=Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/horizontal stone chamber with frontal entrance|13=User:Immanuelle/Side-entrance Sarcophagus Chamber|qq=}} opening to the south. The chamber is constructed using tuff-cut stones, with a rectangular floor plan and a trapezoidal cross-section. The interior likely contained a lacquered wooden coffin. burial goods such as human bones (teeth), iron nails, and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} were discovered during the excavation.

The tomb is dated to the late {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}}, making it a representative example of the end of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. Based on its location, map_size, and construction, it is thought to be the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|WD=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe|1=Prince Kusakabe}}, Crown Prince to Emperor Tenmu.

== burial ==
The burial chamber features a unique horizontal stone structure:
* '''area:''' Length 3.06m; width 2.12m; estimated height 2.50m.
* '''Material:''' Rectangular tuff blocks measuring 50cm × 50cm × 30cm.
* '''built:''' The walls were built vertically up to a height of 1.27m, after which they were inclined inward at approximately 60 degrees, creating a trapezoidal cross-section.
* '''Interior:''' The floor was paved with two layers of stone, and a section of the floor was coated with plaster. 

burial goods recovered include more than 50 iron nails, lacquer fragments, and coffin fittings, suggesting the use of a lacquered wooden coffin. The presence of large nails implies that the coffin rested on a wooden plattype.

== Historical Context ==
The tomb is believed to be the burial site of {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|WD=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe|1=Prince Kusakabe}}, who served as Crown Prince to Emperor Tenmu. Prince Kusakabe died prematurely in 689 AD, leaving a significant legacy through his descendants, including Emperor Monmu and Empress Genshō.

While the site is not officially Historic Site1 as a historic landmark, its map_size, construction, and historical significance make it an important archaeological site. The burial chamber has been reconstructed and is on display at the {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|WD=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute|1=The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture}} Museum.

== Related Facilities ==
* {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|WD=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute|1=The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture}} Museum (Kashihara, Nara Prefecture): Reconstructed burial chamber from Tsukamyojin Kofun is on display.
